question: What are the core features of the Docling package as described in the report?
ground_truth: Docling is an easy-to-use, self-contained, open-source package for PDF document conversion, powered by specialized AI models for layout analysis (DocLayNet) and table structure recognition (TableFormer). It runs efficiently on commodity hardware, has a flexible code interface for extensibility, and is licensed under MIT.
contexts: This technical report introduces _Docling_ , an easy to use, self-contained, MITlicensed open-source package for PDF document conversion. It is powered by state-of-the-art specialized AI models for layout analysis (DocLayNet) and table structure recognition (TableFormer), and runs efficiently on commodity hardware in a small resource budget. The code interface allows for easy extensibility and addition of new features and models.

question: What are some key features of the open-source document conversion tool Docling as described in the text?
ground_truth: Docling is an open-source Python library that converts PDF documents into JSON or Markdown format, understanding detailed page layout, reading order, figures, and table structures, extracting metadata, optionally applying OCR, and can be configured for batch or interactive modes with support for accelerators like GPU.
contexts:
Converting PDF documents back into a machine-processable format has been a major challenge for decades due to their huge variability in formats, weak standardization and printing-optimized characteristic, which discards most structural features and metadata. With the advent of LLMs and popular application patterns such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), leveraging the rich content embedded in PDFs has become ever more relevant. In the past decade, several powerful document understanding solutions have emerged on the market, most of which are commercial software, cloud offerings [3] and most recently, multi-modal vision-language models. As of today, only a handful of open-source tools cover PDF conversion, leaving a significant feature and quality gap to proprietary solutions.  
With _Docling_ , we open-source a very capable and efficient document conversion tool which builds on the powerful, specialized AI models and datasets for layout analysis and table structure recognition we developed and presented in the recent past [12, 13, 9]. Docling is designed as a simple, self-contained python library with permissive license, running entirely locally on commodity hardware. Its code architecture allows for easy extensibility and addition of new features and models.  
Docling Technical Report  
1  
Here is what Docling delivers today:  
- Converts PDF documents to JSON or Markdown format, stable and lightning fast  
- Understands detailed page layout, reading order, locates figures and recovers table structures  
- Extracts metadata from the document, such as title, authors, references and language  
- Optionally applies OCR, e.g. for scanned PDFs  
- Can be configured to be optimal for batch-mode (i.e high throughput, low time-to-solution) or interactive mode (compromise on efficiency, low time-to-solution)  
- Can leverage different accelerators (GPU, MPS, etc).

question: How can you convert a PDF document to Markdown format using Docling?
ground_truth: You can convert a PDF document to Markdown format using Docling by creating an instance of the DocumentConverter class, using the convert_single method with the PDF source URL or path, and then calling the render_as_markdown method on the result.
contexts:
To use Docling, you can simply install the _docling_ package from PyPI. Documentation and examples are available in our GitHub repository at github.com/DS4SD/docling. All required model assets[1] are downloaded to a local huggingface datasets cache on first use, unless you choose to pre-install the model assets in advance.  
Docling provides an easy code interface to convert PDF documents from file system, URLs or binary streams, and retrieve the output in either JSON or Markdown format. For convenience, separate methods are offered to convert single documents or batches of documents. A basic usage example is illustrated below. Further examples are available in the Doclign code repository.  
```
fromdocling. document_converterimportDocumentConverter
source="https :// arxiv.org/pdf /2206.01062"#PDFpathorURL
converter=DocumentConverter ()
result=converter. convert_single (source)
print(result. render_as_markdown ())#output:"##DocLayNet:ALarge
Human -AnnotatedDatasetforDocument -LayoutAnalysis[...]"
```  
Optionally, you can configure custom pipeline features and runtime options, such as turning on or off features (e.g. OCR, table structure recognition), enforcing limits on the input document size, and defining the budget of CPU threads. Advanced usage examples and options are documented in the README file. Docling also provides a _Dockerfile_ to demonstrate how to install and run it inside a container.

question: What are the main steps in Docling's linear pipeline for processing documents?
ground_truth: The main steps in Docling's linear pipeline are: first, parsing the document with a PDF backend to retrieve text tokens and page images; second, applying AI models independently on each page to extract features and content; and finally, aggregating results from all pages, post-processing to augment metadata and detect language, and assembling a typed document object for serialization.
contexts: Docling implements a linear pipeline of operations, which execute sequentially on each given document (see Fig. 1). Each document is first parsed by a PDF backend, which retrieves the programmatic text tokens, consisting of string content and its coordinates on the page, and also renders a bitmap image of each page to support downstream operations. Then, the standard model pipeline applies a sequence of AI models independently on every page in the document to extract features and content, such as layout and table structures. Finally, the results from all pages are aggregated and passed through a post-processing stage, which augments metadata, detects the document language, infers reading-order and eventually assembles a typed document object which can be serialized to JSON or Markdown.

question: What are the two key requirements that the PDF backend in Docling aims to fulfill?
ground_truth: The two key requirements are: a) to retrieve all text content along with their geometric coordinates on each page, and b) to render the visual representation of each page as it would appear in a PDF viewer.
contexts:
Two basic requirements to process PDF documents in our pipeline are a) to retrieve all text content and their geometric coordinates on each page and b) to render the visual representation of each page as it would appear in a PDF viewer. Both these requirements are encapsulated in Docling’s PDF backend interface. While there are several open-source PDF parsing libraries available for python, we faced major obstacles with all of them for different reasons, among which were restrictive > 1 see huggingface.co/ds4sd/docling-models/ 2 **==> picture [396 x 140] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
{;}
Assemble results, Serialize as
Apply document JSON
post-processing or Markdown

PDF pages Analysis Structure
Model Pipeline
**----- End of picture text -----**
Figure 1: Sketch of Docling’s default processing pipeline. The inner part of the model pipeline is easily customizable and extensible. licensing (e.g. pymupdf [7]), poor speed or unrecoverable quality issues, such as merged text cells across far-apart text tokens or table columns (pypdfium, PyPDF) [15, 14]. We therefore decided to provide multiple backend choices, and additionally open-source a custombuilt PDF parser, which is based on the low-level _qpdf_ [4] library. It is made available in a separate package named _docling-parse_ and powers the default PDF backend in Docling. As an alternative, we provide a PDF backend relying on _pypdfium_ , which may be a safe backup choice in certain cases, e.g. if issues are seen with particular font encodings.

question: What are the two AI models released by Docling as part of their open-source initiative?
ground_truth: The two AI models released by Docling are a layout analysis model, which is an accurate object-detector for page elements, and TableFormer, a state-of-the-art table structure recognition model.
contexts: As part of Docling, we initially release two highly capable AI models to the open-source community, which have been developed and published recently by our team. The first model is a layout analysis model, an accurate object-detector for page elements [13]. The second model is TableFormer [12, 9], a state-of-the-art table structure recognition model. We provide the pre-trained weights (hosted on huggingface) and a separate package for the inference code as _docling-ibm-models_ . Both models are also powering the open-access deepsearch-experience, our cloud-native service for knowledge exploration tasks.

question: What technology does the layout analysis model utilize for inference, and what is the source of its architecture?
ground_truth: The layout analysis model uses _onnxruntime_ for inference, and its architecture is derived from RT-DETR, retrained on the DocLayNet dataset and proprietary datasets.
contexts:
Our layout analysis model is an object-detector which predicts the bounding-boxes and classes of various elements on the image of a given page. Its architecture is derived from RT-DETR [16] and re-trained on DocLayNet [13], our popular human-annotated dataset for document-layout analysis, among other proprietary datasets. For inference, our implementation relies on the _onnxruntime_ [5].  
The Docling pipeline feeds page images at 72 dpi resolution, which can be processed on a single CPU with sub-second latency. All predicted bounding-box proposals for document elements are post-processed to remove overlapping proposals based on confidence and size, and then intersected with the text tokens in the PDF to group them into meaningful and complete units such as paragraphs, section titles, list items, captions, figures or tables.

question: What are some of the characteristics of tables that the TableFormer model can handle?
ground_truth: TableFormer can handle tables with partial or no borderlines, empty cells, rows or columns, cell spans, hierarchy at column-header or row-header levels, inconsistent indentation or alignment, and other complexities.
contexts:
The TableFormer model [12], first published in 2022 and since refined with a custom structure token language [9], is a vision-transformer model for table structure recovery. It can predict the logical row and column structure of a given table based on an input image, and determine which table cells belong to column headers, row headers or the table body. Compared to earlier approaches, TableFormer handles many characteristics of tables, such as partial or no borderlines, empty cells, rows or columns, cell spans and hierarchy both on column-heading or row-heading level, tables with inconsistent indentation or alignment and other complexities. For inference, our implementation relies on _PyTorch_ [2].  
3  
The Docling pipeline feeds all table objects detected in the layout analysis to the TableFormer model, by providing an image-crop of the table and the included text cells. TableFormer structure predictions are matched back to the PDF cells in post-processing to avoid expensive re-transcription text in the table image. Typical tables require between 2 and 6 seconds to be processed on a standard CPU, strongly depending on the amount of included table cells.

question: What OCR library does Docling use in its initial release, and what are some of its characteristics?
ground_truth: In its initial release, Docling uses the EasyOCR library, which is a popular third-party OCR library supporting many languages. It processes high-resolution page images at 216 dpi to capture small print details and provides reasonable transcription quality, though it can be slow on CPU, taking over 30 seconds per page.
contexts:
Docling provides optional support for OCR, for example to cover scanned PDFs or content in bitmaps images embedded on a page. In our initial release, we rely on _EasyOCR_ [1], a popular thirdparty OCR library with support for many languages. Docling, by default, feeds a high-resolution page image (216 dpi) to the OCR engine, to allow capturing small print detail in decent quality. While EasyOCR delivers reasonable transcription quality, we observe that it runs fairly slow on CPU (upwards of 30 seconds per page).  
We are actively seeking collaboration from the open-source community to extend Docling with additional OCR backends and speed improvements.

question: What does Docling do with its prediction results in the final pipeline stage?
ground_truth: In the final pipeline stage, Docling assembles all prediction results into a well-defined datatype representing a converted document, then passes this document object through a post-processing model to enhance features such as detecting the language, correcting reading order, matching figures with captions, and labeling metadata, before optionally serializing it to JSON or converting it into Markdown.
contexts: In the final pipeline stage, Docling assembles all prediction results produced on each page into a well-defined datatype that encapsulates a converted document, as defined in the auxiliary package _docling-core_ . The generated document object is passed through a post-processing model which leverages several algorithms to augment features, such as detection of the document language, correcting the reading order, matching figures with captions and labelling metadata such as title, authors and references. The final output can then be serialized to JSON or transformed into a Markdown representation at the users request.

question: What is required of implementations of model classes in Docling in terms of Python interface?
ground_truth: Implementations of model classes in Docling must satisfy the Python `Callable` interface, with the `__call__` method accepting an iterator over page objects and returning an iterator over page objects augmented with additional features as predicted by the model.
contexts:
Docling provides a straight-forward interface to extend its capabilities, namely the model pipeline. A model pipeline constitutes the central part in the processing, following initial document parsing and preceding output assembly, and can be fully customized by sub-classing from an abstract baseclass ( _BaseModelPipeline_ ) or cloning the default model pipeline. This effectively allows to fully customize the chain of models, add or replace models, and introduce additional pipeline configuration parameters. To use a custom model pipeline, the custom pipeline class to instantiate can be provided as an argument to the main document conversion methods. We invite everyone in the community to propose additional or alternative models and improvements.  
Implementations of model classes must satisfy the python `Callable` interface. The `__call__` method must accept an iterator over page objects, and produce another iterator over the page objects which were augmented with the additional features predicted by the model, by extending the provided `PagePredictions` data model accordingly.

question: What are the two hardware systems used for testing Docling's processing speed, and what software environments do they run?
ground_truth: The two hardware systems used are a MacBook Pro M3 Max and a bare-metal server running Ubuntu 20.04 LTS on an Intel Xeon E5-2690 CPU.
contexts:
In this section, we establish some reference numbers for the processing speed of Docling and the resource budget it requires. All tests in this section are run with default options on our standard test set distributed with Docling, which consists of three papers from arXiv and two IBM Redbooks, with a total of 225 pages. Measurements were taken using both available PDF backends on two different hardware systems: one MacBook Pro M3 Max, and one bare-metal server running Ubuntu 20.04 LTS on an Intel Xeon E5-2690 CPU. For reproducibility, we fixed the thread budget (through setting _OMP_ ~~_N_~~ _UM_ ~~_T_~~ _HREADS environment variable_ ) once to 4 (Docling default) and once to 16 (equal to full core count on the test hardware). All results are shown in Table 1. If you need to run Docling in very low-resource environments, please consider configuring the pypdfium backend. While it is faster and more memory efficient than the default _docling-parse_ backend, it will come at the expense of worse quality results, especially in table structure recovery. Establishing GPU acceleration support for the AI models is currently work-in-progress and largely untested, but may work implicitly when CUDA is available and discovered by the onnxruntime and 4 torch runtimes backing the Docling pipeline. We will deliver updates on this topic at in a future version of this report. Table 1: Runtime characteristics of Docling with the standard model pipeline and settings, on our test dataset of 225 pages, on two different systems. OCR is disabled. We show the time-to-solution (TTS), computed throughput in pages per second, and the peak memory used (resident set size) for both the Docling-native PDF backend and for the pypdfium backend, using 4 and 16 threads. |CPU

question: What is the purpose of the open-source package called _quackling_ in relation to Docling?
ground_truth: The open-source package _quackling_ utilizes Docling's feature-rich document output to enable document-native optimized vector embedding and chunking, facilitating applications like retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and integrating seamlessly with LlamaIndex.
contexts: Thanks to the high-quality, richly structured document conversion achieved by Docling, its output qualifies for numerous downstream applications. For example, Docling can provide a base for detailed enterprise document search, passage retrieval or classification use-cases, or support knowledge extraction pipelines, allowing specific treatment of different structures in the document, such as tables, figures, section structure or references. For popular generative AI application patterns, such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), we provide _quackling_ , an open-source package which capitalizes on Docling’s feature-rich document output to enable document-native optimized vector embedding and chunking. It plugs in seamlessly with LLM frameworks such as LlamaIndex [8]. Since Docling is fast, stable and cheap to run, it also makes for an excellent choice to build document-derived datasets. With its powerful table structure recognition, it provides significant benefit to automated knowledge-base construction [11, 10]. Docling is also integrated within the open IBM data prep kit [6], which implements scalable data transforms to build large-scale multi-modal training datasets.

question: What future models are planned to be added to Docling as mentioned in the text?
ground_truth: The planned future models to be added to Docling include a figure-classifier model, an equation-recognition model, a code-recognition model, and more.
contexts:
Docling is designed to allow easy extension of the model library and pipelines. In the future, we plan to extend Docling with several more models, such as a figure-classifier model, an equationrecognition model, a code-recognition model and more. This will help improve the quality of conversion for specific types of content, as well as augment extracted document metadata with additional information. Further investment into testing and optimizing GPU acceleration as well as improving the Docling-native PDF backend are on our roadmap, too.  
**We encourage everyone to propose or implement additional features and models, and will gladly take your inputs and contributions under review** . The codebase of Docling is open for use and contribution, under the MIT license agreement and in alignment with our contributing guidelines included in the Docling repository. If you use Docling in your projects, please consider citing this technical report.

question: What is the primary purpose of the _Docling_ package, and what are its key technological features?
ground_truth: The primary purpose of _Docling_ is to facilitate PDF document conversion through an easy-to-use, open-source tool. Its key features include leveraging advanced AI models for layout analysis and table recognition, being resource-efficient enough to run on standard hardware, and offering an extensible code interface for adding new functionalities.
contexts: This technical report introduces _Docling_ , an easy to use, self-contained, MITlicensed open-source package for PDF document conversion. It is powered by state-of-the-art specialized AI models for layout analysis (DocLayNet) and table structure recognition (TableFormer), and runs efficiently on commodity hardware in a small resource budget. The code interface allows for easy extensibility and addition of new features and models.

question: What are some of the main capabilities of the open-source tool Docling in handling PDF documents?
ground_truth: Docling can convert PDFs into JSON or Markdown, understand page layouts and reading order, locate figures, recover table structures, extract metadata, optionally apply OCR, and be configured for different processing modes with support for hardware accelerators.
contexts:
Converting PDF documents back into a machine-processable format has been a major challenge for decades due to their huge variability in formats, weak standardization and printing-optimized characteristic, which discards most structural features and metadata. With the advent of LLMs and popular application patterns such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), leveraging the rich content embedded in PDFs has become ever more relevant. In the past decade, several powerful document understanding solutions have emerged on the market, most of which are commercial software, cloud offerings [3] and most recently, multi-modal vision-language models. As of today, only a handful of open-source tools cover PDF conversion, leaving a significant feature and quality gap to proprietary solutions.  
With _Docling_ , we open-source a very capable and efficient document conversion tool which builds on the powerful, specialized AI models and datasets for layout analysis and table structure recognition we developed and presented in the recent past [12, 13, 9]. Docling is designed as a simple, self-contained python library with permissive license, running entirely locally on commodity hardware. Its code architecture allows for easy extensibility and addition of new features and models.  
Docling Technical Report  
1  
Here is what Docling delivers today:  
- Converts PDF documents to JSON or Markdown format, stable and lightning fast  
- Understands detailed page layout, reading order, locates figures and recovers table structures  
- Extracts metadata from the document, such as title, authors, references and language  
- Optionally applies OCR, e.g. for scanned PDFs  
- Can be configured to be optimal for batch-mode (i.e high throughput, low time-to-solution) or interactive mode (compromise on efficiency, low time-to-solution)  
- Can leverage different accelerators (GPU, MPS, etc).

question: What are the steps to install and begin using Docling for document conversion tasks, and what customization options are available for its processing pipeline?
ground_truth: To start using Docling, you need to install the package from PyPI using a package manager like pip. Once installed, you can use its simple interface to convert documents from various sources such as files, URLs, or streams into JSON or Markdown formats. It offers methods for processing individual documents or batches. Customization options include enabling or disabling features like OCR or table recognition, setting input size limits, and adjusting CPU thread usage, allowing users to tailor the processing pipeline to their needs.
contexts:
To use Docling, you can simply install the _docling_ package from PyPI. Documentation and examples are available in our GitHub repository at github.com/DS4SD/docling. All required model assets[1] are downloaded to a local huggingface datasets cache on first use, unless you choose to pre-install the model assets in advance.  
Docling provides an easy code interface to convert PDF documents from file system, URLs or binary streams, and retrieve the output in either JSON or Markdown format. For convenience, separate methods are offered to convert single documents or batches of documents. A basic usage example is illustrated below. Further examples are available in the Doclign code repository.  
```
fromdocling. document_converterimportDocumentConverter
source="https :// arxiv.org/pdf /2206.01062"#PDFpathorURL
converter=DocumentConverter ()
result=converter. convert_single (source)
print(result. render_as_markdown ())#output:"##DocLayNet:ALarge
Human -AnnotatedDatasetforDocument -LayoutAnalysis[...]"
```  
Optionally, you can configure custom pipeline features and runtime options, such as turning on or off features (e.g. OCR, table structure recognition), enforcing limits on the input document size, and defining the budget of CPU threads. Advanced usage examples and options are documented in the README file. Docling also provides a _Dockerfile_ to demonstrate how to install and run it inside a container.

question: What are the main steps in the process that Docling uses to analyze a document?
ground_truth: Docling first parses the document to extract text tokens and render page images, then it applies AI models to analyze layout and structures on each page, and finally it aggregates the findings, enriches metadata, detects language, determines reading order, and creates a structured document object.
contexts: Docling implements a linear pipeline of operations, which execute sequentially on each given document (see Fig. 1). Each document is first parsed by a PDF backend, which retrieves the programmatic text tokens, consisting of string content and its coordinates on the page, and also renders a bitmap image of each page to support downstream operations. Then, the standard model pipeline applies a sequence of AI models independently on every page in the document to extract features and content, such as layout and table structures. Finally, the results from all pages are aggregated and passed through a post-processing stage, which augments metadata, detects the document language, infers reading-order and eventually assembles a typed document object which can be serialized to JSON or Markdown.

question: What are the two main capabilities required in the PDF processing pipeline described, and how does Docling address the challenges faced with third-party libraries?
ground_truth: The two main capabilities required are extracting all text content with their geometric coordinates and rendering the page visually as in a PDF viewer. To address problems like licensing restrictions, performance issues, and quality concerns encountered with existing open-source libraries, Docling implements multiple backend options and develops a custom PDF parser based on the _qpdf_ library, providing more reliable and flexible processing.
contexts:
Two basic requirements to process PDF documents in our pipeline are a) to retrieve all text content and their geometric coordinates on each page and b) to render the visual representation of each page as it would appear in a PDF viewer. Both these requirements are encapsulated in Docling’s PDF backend interface. While there are several open-source PDF parsing libraries available for python, we faced major obstacles with all of them for different reasons, among which were restrictive > 1 see huggingface.co/ds4sd/docling-models/ 2 **==> picture [396 x 140] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

Figure 1: Sketch of Docling’s default processing pipeline. The inner part of the model pipeline is easily customizable and extensible. licensing (e.g. pymupdf [7]), poor speed or unrecoverable quality issues, such as merged text cells across far-apart text tokens or table columns (pypdfium, PyPDF) [15, 14]. We therefore decided to provide multiple backend choices, and additionally open-source a custombuilt PDF parser, which is based on the low-level _qpdf_ [4] library. It is made available in a separate package named _docling-parse_ and powers the default PDF backend in Docling. As an alternative, we provide a PDF backend relying on _pypdfium_ , which may be a safe backup choice in certain cases, e.g. if issues are seen with particular font encodings.

question: What are the two primary AI models released by Docling for open-source use, and what are their main functions?
ground_truth: The two AI models released by Docling are a layout analysis model for detecting page elements and TableFormer, a model for recognizing table structures.
contexts: As part of Docling, we initially release two highly capable AI models to the open-source community, which have been developed and published recently by our team. The first model is a layout analysis model, an accurate object-detector for page elements [13]. The second model is TableFormer [12, 9], a state-of-the-art table structure recognition model. We provide the pre-trained weights (hosted on huggingface) and a separate package for the inference code as _docling-ibm-models_ . Both models are also powering the open-access deepsearch-experience, our cloud-native service for knowledge exploration tasks.

question: What is the primary function of the layout analysis model described, and what are some key aspects of its architecture and inference process?
ground_truth: The layout analysis model identifies and classifies various elements in document images by predicting their bounding boxes and classes. It is based on the RT-DETR architecture, trained on a dataset called DocLayNet, and uses onnxruntime for inference. The process involves resizing page images, quickly processing them on a CPU, removing overlapping predictions, and aligning them with PDF text tokens to organize content into meaningful units like paragraphs and figures.
contexts:
Our layout analysis model is an object-detector which predicts the bounding-boxes and classes of various elements on the image of a given page. Its architecture is derived from RT-DETR [16] and re-trained on DocLayNet [13], our popular human-annotated dataset for document-layout analysis, among other proprietary datasets. For inference, our implementation relies on the _onnxruntime_ [5].  
The Docling pipeline feeds page images at 72 dpi resolution, which can be processed on a single CPU with sub-second latency. All predicted bounding-box proposals for document elements are post-processed to remove overlapping proposals based on confidence and size, and then intersected with the text tokens in the PDF to group them into meaningful and complete units such as paragraphs, section titles, list items, captions, figures or tables.

question: What is the primary function of the TableFormer model, and what are some of the complexities it can handle in tables?
ground_truth: TableFormer is a vision-transformer model designed to recover the structure of tables from images, capable of predicting row and column organization, and classifying table cells. It can handle various complexities including tables with missing or partial borders, empty cells, spanning cells, hierarchical headers, inconsistent alignments, and other irregularities.
contexts:
The TableFormer model [12], first published in 2022 and since refined with a custom structure token language [9], is a vision-transformer model for table structure recovery. It can predict the logical row and column structure of a given table based on an input image, and determine which table cells belong to column headers, row headers or the table body. Compared to earlier approaches, TableFormer handles many characteristics of tables, such as partial or no borderlines, empty cells, rows or columns, cell spans and hierarchy both on column-heading or row-heading level, tables with inconsistent indentation or alignment and other complexities. For inference, our implementation relies on _PyTorch_ [2].  
3  
The Docling pipeline feeds all table objects detected in the layout analysis to the TableFormer model, by providing an image-crop of the table and the included text cells. TableFormer structure predictions are matched back to the PDF cells in post-processing to avoid expensive re-transcription text in the table image. Typical tables require between 2 and 6 seconds to be processed on a standard CPU, strongly depending on the amount of included table cells.

question: What is a key challenge associated with using EasyOCR in Docling, and how is the team planning to address this issue?
ground_truth: The main challenge is that EasyOCR tends to run slowly on CPU, taking over 30 seconds per page. The team plans to seek community collaboration to integrate alternative OCR tools and enhance processing speed.
contexts:
Docling provides optional support for OCR, for example to cover scanned PDFs or content in bitmaps images embedded on a page. In our initial release, we rely on _EasyOCR_ [1], a popular thirdparty OCR library with support for many languages. Docling, by default, feeds a high-resolution page image (216 dpi) to the OCR engine, to allow capturing small print detail in decent quality. While EasyOCR delivers reasonable transcription quality, we observe that it runs fairly slow on CPU (upwards of 30 seconds per page).  
We are actively seeking collaboration from the open-source community to extend Docling with additional OCR backends and speed improvements.

question: What is the purpose of the post-processing model in the final stage of Docling's pipeline?
ground_truth: The post-processing model enhances the initial predictions by adding features like language detection, correcting reading order, pairing figures with captions, and labeling metadata such as titles and authors.
contexts: In the final pipeline stage, Docling assembles all prediction results produced on each page into a well-defined datatype that encapsulates a converted document, as defined in the auxiliary package _docling-core_ . The generated document object is passed through a post-processing model which leverages several algorithms to augment features, such as detection of the document language, correcting the reading order, matching figures with captions and labelling metadata such as title, authors and references. The final output can then be serialized to JSON or transformed into a Markdown representation at the users request.

question: What is the primary purpose of customizing the model pipeline in Docling, and how can it be achieved?
ground_truth: The main purpose of customizing the model pipeline in Docling is to modify or enhance the sequence of processing models, allowing for additions, replacements, or new configurations of models. This can be achieved by subclassing the abstract _BaseModelPipeline_ class or cloning the default pipeline, and then providing the custom class as an argument to the document conversion methods.
contexts:
Docling provides a straight-forward interface to extend its capabilities, namely the model pipeline. A model pipeline constitutes the central part in the processing, following initial document parsing and preceding output assembly, and can be fully customized by sub-classing from an abstract baseclass ( _BaseModelPipeline_ ) or cloning the default model pipeline. This effectively allows to fully customize the chain of models, add or replace models, and introduce additional pipeline configuration parameters. To use a custom model pipeline, the custom pipeline class to instantiate can be provided as an argument to the main document conversion methods. We invite everyone in the community to propose additional or alternative models and improvements.  
Implementations of model classes must satisfy the python `Callable` interface. The `__call__` method must accept an iterator over page objects, and produce another iterator over the page objects which were augmented with the additional features predicted by the model, by extending the provided `PagePredictions` data model accordingly.

question: Based on the provided data, how does changing the thread allocation from 4 to 16 affect the processing time, pages per second, and memory usage for the native backend on the Apple M3 Max system?
ground_truth: Increasing the thread count from 4 to 16 on the Apple M3 Max system reduces the processing time from 177 seconds to 167 seconds, improves pages per second from 1.27 to 1.34, and decreases memory usage from 6.20 GB to 2.56 GB.
contexts:
In this section, we establish some reference numbers for the processing speed of Docling and the resource budget it requires. All tests in this section are run with default options on our standard test set distributed with Docling, which consists of three papers from arXiv and two IBM Redbooks, with a total of 225 pages. Measurements were taken using both available PDF backends on two different hardware systems: one MacBook Pro M3 Max, and one bare-metal server running Ubuntu 20.04 LTS on an Intel Xeon E5-2690 CPU. For reproducibility, we fixed the thread budget (through setting _OMP_ ~~_N_~~ _UM_ ~~_T_~~ _HREADS environment variable_ ) once to 4 (Docling default) and once to 16 (equal to full core count on the test hardware). All results are shown in Table 1. If you need to run Docling in very low-resource environments, please consider configuring the pypdfium backend. While it is faster and more memory efficient than the default _docling-parse_ backend, it will come at the expense of worse quality results, especially in table structure recovery. Establishing GPU acceleration support for the AI models is currently work-in-progress and largely untested, but may work implicitly when CUDA is available and discovered by the onnxruntime and 4 torch runtimes backing the Docling pipeline. We will deliver updates on this topic at in a future version of this report. Table 1: Runtime characteristics of Docling with the standard model pipeline and settings, on our test dataset of 225 pages, on two different systems. OCR is disabled. We show the time-to-solution (TTS), computed throughput in pages per second, and the peak memory used (resident set size) for both the Docling-native PDF backend and for the pypdfium backend, using 4 and 16 threads. |CPU

question: What are some of the downstream applications and uses enabled by Docling's high-quality document conversion capabilities?
ground_truth: Docling's advanced document conversion facilitates applications such as enterprise document searching, passage retrieval, classification, knowledge extraction, and building datasets. It also supports AI patterns like retrieval-augmented generation through its open-source package quackling, integrates with LLM frameworks, and aids in automated knowledge-base construction and large-scale data processing.
contexts: Thanks to the high-quality, richly structured document conversion achieved by Docling, its output qualifies for numerous downstream applications. For example, Docling can provide a base for detailed enterprise document search, passage retrieval or classification use-cases, or support knowledge extraction pipelines, allowing specific treatment of different structures in the document, such as tables, figures, section structure or references. For popular generative AI application patterns, such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), we provide _quackling_ , an open-source package which capitalizes on Docling’s feature-rich document output to enable document-native optimized vector embedding and chunking. It plugs in seamlessly with LLM frameworks such as LlamaIndex [8]. Since Docling is fast, stable and cheap to run, it also makes for an excellent choice to build document-derived datasets. With its powerful table structure recognition, it provides significant benefit to automated knowledge-base construction [11, 10]. Docling is also integrated within the open IBM data prep kit [6], which implements scalable data transforms to build large-scale multi-modal training datasets.

question: What future enhancements and contributions are planned for the Docling project, and how does it support community involvement?
ground_truth: Future plans for Docling include adding new models such as a figure classifier, equation recognition, and code recognition to improve content conversion and metadata extraction. The project also aims to enhance GPU performance and the PDF backend. It encourages community contributions by making its code open-source under the MIT license and welcoming proposals and implementations of additional features.
contexts:
Docling is designed to allow easy extension of the model library and pipelines. In the future, we plan to extend Docling with several more models, such as a figure-classifier model, an equationrecognition model, a code-recognition model and more. This will help improve the quality of conversion for specific types of content, as well as augment extracted document metadata with additional information. Further investment into testing and optimizing GPU acceleration as well as improving the Docling-native PDF backend are on our roadmap, too.  
**We encourage everyone to propose or implement additional features and models, and will gladly take your inputs and contributions under review** . The codebase of Docling is open for use and contribution, under the MIT license agreement and in alignment with our contributing guidelines included in the Docling repository. If you use Docling in your projects, please consider citing this technical report.

question: Which open-source project provides tools for converting PDF documents without altering their content, and where can its source code be found?
ground_truth: The project is Qpdf, and its source code is available at https://github.com/qpdf/qpdf.
